speaker: interviewer
text: Morris Perlmutter. January 4th, 1996. Before we go forward, is there anything else you want to add about Cracow?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Cracow-- the pogrom in Cracow, I did not see it. But I was there, next day I heard about it. And that put a big scare of all the kibbutzim we were there. Because there was many kibbutzim. Because in Cracow we were at that time because [NON-ENGLISH] was in Mudova, Mudova was a very Jewish section before the war. There was a big Yeshiva in Mudova. There was a big synagogue right next to it. Cracow--

speaker: interviewee
text: So most of the Jews wanted to leave. They were run out from Cracow. And the pogrom put up big damper on it. Which it happened actually where my mother lived, which it was in [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] if anybody knows between Cracow, [NON-ENGLISH is right across from the [NON-ENGLISH]. OK, the [NON-ENGLISH] is something else, a palace. Was your mother there at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was in [NON-ENGLISH]. In that house, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she affected?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, no. I really don't know who really got hurt in that time. I don't know. We-- we went away right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: The kibbutz left, and then we went to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So now you're in Germany, in Pocking.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in Pocking, that was outside of Munchen. And after a few weeks, or just a couple of weeks, I wanted to see my mother. I wanted know what happened to her. So I left the kibbutz, and I was on my way back. On the train, which we had to go, I knew my mother was in Bad Rechenhall. And on the train to Bad Reichenhall, it stops on one side, and we have to go down to take the next train to--

speaker: interviewee
text: On the way down when I stopped and we went down, coming up on the steps, my mother is coming. I said, where are you going? She's going to me. Where are you going? I'm going to you. She says, where are we going? We're going to you. So I left the kibbutz. I took my mother back where she stayed. And I spent two years in Bad Reichenhall. Bad Reichenhall is south of Germany. It's not far from the border of Austria. Over there, they-- in the camp they used to train for Haganah. They had a lot of people there for the Israeli already, the Israeli groups. They used to have a theater there. They had all kinds of things for the last two years. So what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to school. I went to school in Hebrew. I was-- I went to a school for a trade school. I was learning to become a tool and dye man. And tool and dye, which are on the machines. I was very good at it actually, and all that. And that was it. And on the end of it, every camp used to have a list of people, survivors. All the time, used to have a list. And all of a sudden, I went through the list and I found my friends from the 100 children from Zakopane, from Cracow. And they were in a-- not far, in a camp which the city called Rosenheim. And the first thing I did is get on the train and go there. And I found in a kibbutz, but which not a religious kibbutz. And they were in a different kibbutz, and they met quite a few of the older guys and girls from-- from the-- from Zakopane and Cracow. And they were planning to go to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: And me, I says, if I could go? And they says, you alone can come. But your mother, no. We take only young people, ready to fight, ready to go, and that's that. And I talked to the [NON-ENGLISH] of the kibbutz, and the man of the kibbutz in charge. He liked me and everything else. And he says, you're our friends, you're like brothers, and all that. You can come with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went back to my mother. And I told her that I'm going to go. And I says, you have three sisters and two brothers in United States and Canada. For you alone, you're a sick woman. Because she used to be-- in camps she used to be most of the time in the hospital. She was all the time sick. She was a woman that she was never felt very well.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I says, you go, they'll look after you, and I'll go to Israel. And she cried, we'll go together. If not, I'm going to go with you. I says, no, I'm going to fight, and all that. And we were ready. I was trained already a little bit and all that. And I says, no. And she agreed with me. When was this? What year was this?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1947-- yeah. The beginning of '48, something like that. And what happened. I went back to the kibbutz to go with them. I came back and they were gone. So I said, I believe in destiny, [NON-ENGLISH] in Yiddish. I will go with my mother. And it actually turned out that way. So I went back to Bad Reichenhall and I said, I'll go with you to Canada. We moved to the camp in Munchen, arranged the papers there. We stayed in Munchen I think for a few months. And we got the papers in June of the-- oh, June. Yeah, I think it took us two of the weeks to get here. We arrived in-- in the 16th of June to Halifax. One of those army boats with six or five high bunkers. And we came to Halifax. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Another thing I can mention, that if I would have gone with the guys, they wind up to be on exodus. And they were cut, and they were not exodus, which in the movie. Because the exodus in the movie comes to Israel. The exodus that came, 5,500 people, they were shipped back to Hamburg, to Germany. And then they went back to Israel. And a couple of them were killed in 1948 in Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to Canada because-- with my mother. And I came to Toronto. And I knew only about one sister here of my mother. And actually the other two sisters and a brother from States were here, and everything else. And after a few days, they start arguing, who's going to look after those two? I says, I survived, and you're worrying about looking after us? Look after your one sister. Five sisters were killed. I screamed. And they didn't answer. I never saw them again. The American-- I only saw the one, my Auntie in Canada. But in the United States I never saw them again. I never know my family there. Because they had a lot of children. I met one time or something one of them. But they didn't want-- I fought with them. And I saw that it wasn't so-- they didn't appreciate we came. Were they interested in what you'd been through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody wanted to know. I did not say what I went through. My life, I don't care. I wanted to know, they had five more sisters or brothers. What happened to them?

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't ask?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't ask a question. Even if we would have told, we don't want to know to tell. By accident, I found an uncle here. My father's brother was in Canada, which I never knew. He left-- he left in early 1900's he left Russia. At that time was Russia. When I came here and I saw, I wanted to go to learn. One of my cousins, actually, which was nice to me. A few days after I arrived, he took me into EDENS, which now I know. He bought me a suit, and he bought me clothes, and a pair of shoes, and everything else. And he took me to his house, and he took me to the bathtub. And he says, you get a good scrubbing. He took all my old clothes and he threw it out, and I wear new clothes. That's one thing I did get something. I'll never forget. He died already, too. But he did it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I met my uncle here and I didn't know that I had one, like I said, and I looked for a job right away. I want to go to school, but my mother could not [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] realize that there's a full fridge of food. So after she finished eating, she still opened the fridge. And her sister used to say, you're still hungry? She says, I'm not hungry. Don't look. And a lot of times my mother used to say to me she's hungry. I says, no. So I looked for a job. I couldn't get a job. So I went to my uncle and asked him to-- I want to learn to be a baker. He says, oh, no, no, no. He says, OK. I says, I have to get a job. So he gave me a job. I worked 70 hours a week. I got $10 for it. I rented an apartment. Apartment-- actually, a flat. With one bedroom. My mother slept in bedroom. I slept in the living room, what you would call it. Everybody gave me a piece of furniture, gave me a icebox, a piece of ice. And I used to pay $40 a month. And I only made $40 a month. So how can you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: At one time, my mother-- my mother used to know how to cook good. So she found a place that she used to cook a little bit for weddings and simchas. And she used to bring back food. But after a while I got into the union, I started making good money. And two years later, I was in business.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1950-- I arrived in 1948. By the end of '50-- for Christmas, I remember that-- I bought a $30,000 business. And if you're going to say, where I got $30,000? No. Me and my partner, we needed $3,000 to down payment. So I got $1,500 and he got $1,500. I went to my uncle to borrow some money. He says, later on I'll give you. He never wanted to borrow me some. OK. This is what happened in Canada. I made good money in the business. I bought another one, I bought another one. In 1957, I got sick. So I says, what? I survived the war and now I'm going to get an ulcer and all that? I sold everything. I went back to Europe. I bought a car. I traveled through 20 countries. I took my car aboard the Hansel, the Israeli ship. From Napoli, I went to Israel. I stayed in Israel for four months touring the country. Meeting everyone from-- everyone. Almost a lot from the 100 children. Which now-- I didn't meet [NON-ENGLISH]. I wanted to see her many times, but I never got the chance to me her. But you met the others?

speaker: interviewee
text: The others, oh yes. All my friends I met.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your brothers and sister who had moved to Israel before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they accepted me very much. Oh, [NON-ENGLISH]. I came to Israel with a car. When I came off the boat, they didn't know I was coming. And I took the car off and I drove out from Haifa. And they say, how to get to Tel Aviv? He says, choose the ocean is on the right side, you'll get to it. On the way, I picked up a few soldiers, and they took me. And I came and they took me up to the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew the address. But I didn't know the address where he lived, I knew the address where he worked. I came into the big building where he worked and I asked for my brother. He says, he went home. Can you tell me where? So one of his neighbors worked there, so he took me up to my brother's home. He knocked on the door and he says, what do you want? His wife, my sister-in-law started yelling. Why do you bother him now after work? And this and that. And I'm standing there and I'm listening. And then he says to me, shall I tell him? I said, might as well, I'm here. He says, meet your brother. So I met my two brothers and sister in Israel. How long did you stay in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four months. Four months. And I saw Israel very well. And I enjoyed it. I'd like to have lived there, but I still had-- my mother was here. And came back, I opened another business. But I traveled around the world many times, and that's how I spend my money.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the Six-Day War, I came to Israel. And I wanted to stay. And by accident, not far from my sister's house. He told me, you want to meet a girl? And I met a girl. And-- and we-- after two weeks, I said to her-- she says two weeks, I say a week. I says, you want to get married? And she says, I don't know you. I says, it will take 25 years to know me. And she still doesn't know me. So I don't know. And we got married in Israel in October of '69. And we had three children, two children, three. And I got-- I was going to go to the army. And this October war, I supposed to go to the army for the fight, but they wouldn't take me because I was a Canadian. So they asked me to work-- they have a section where they call [NON-ENGLISH]. That's for deliveries of-- to the public and the villages food and everything. I said, all right. I drove a 25 ton truck to the villages to deliver. But at that time, I received a phone call or telegram, I don't remember. My mother died. My mother died in Baycrest.

speaker: interviewee
text: But 1974, the army wanted me to join in. I was already-- I says, why do you want me now? So I went to join in the army, they found out that I had a heart attack. And I didn't know when I had it. They discovered that I had a heart attack, so they gave me off. And I was going down the hill there. I couldn't take it. So I says, I'll go back. I actually did not want to come back to Canada. But only solution for me was to go to a kibbutz. And my wife says, no kibbutz. So I says, OK. I'll go back to Canada. I wanted to go back in business, but I was too sick. I was very, very sick when I came back here. High blood pressure was high. I found a doctor that he really helped me out. Dr. Levy was very good to me. He-- my potassium, my this, he gave me a lot of medicine, and he cured me a lot, but not all of it. I had my heart condition there. So what have you done since you've been back in Canada? Have you worked?

speaker: interviewee
text: Since I've been back is the only thing is I've worked in a bakery. I worked for the baker all the time until about last-- well, in 1992 or 1993 I think. Or '92 or '93, I don't remember, again, I had a severe heart attack in the bakery. And then three months later, I had a quadruple bypass. I went back to work, which I couldn't work anymore. And when I reached 65, I says, that was it. And I went-- and I'm on pension. I don't go back to work. I not well to it. I don't feel-- or don't look sick. But I don't feel like working no more.

speaker: interviewer
text: So just to sum up your story. First of all, is there anything that you would like to add to your story? Anything that I've forgotten to ask you we haven't covered?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only thing I can add is why were they killing? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I collect Judaica. I mean, Judaica in stamps. And I collect everything that Jews have done for the world. And I think they've done quite a bit. And we have to be the scapegoat. So what to add to this? This is my life I passed through. And I'm here, and that's the only thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What message do you want to give your children and your grandchildren based on your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: To my children, I hope it never happens again. And I hope they don't know what it means what I went through. That's the only thing I can say to them. And I said to them, and they know, they know to be strong. They fight back. They learn current day, they learn things, they do not take from nobody nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you want to tell your story today?

speaker: interviewee
text: To tell the story to remains that somebody will listen to it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's not simple to go through two years in [NON-ENGLISH]. That's-- that's it. What else can I say?

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for sharing with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now where do you want me to start? From the-- Start from the right and tell us the names.

speaker: interviewee
text: The top or the sudden?

speaker: interviewer
text: Whichever. Top.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. We start from the right. From the right is my grandfather. Yes. Then--

speaker: interviewer
text: What is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Yossel Perlmutter. Then was my uncle, Abu Perlmutter. And next one, which is holding the baby is Rifka Perlmutter. Next to her is Vedel Perlmutter. The baby she's holding is Gerdle Perlmutter, but not my father's Gerdle. Then above her there's Labish Perlmutter. And there is Panina Perlmutter. And Bonya Perlmutter, Moishe Perlmutter. Now on the bottom sitting is Alex Perlmutter, center. And Werle Perlmutter. And Schuman Perlmutter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that starting from the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the right, yes. Oh, on the top, I went from the left back.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That's all of them. The picture was taken in 1926 in [NON-ENGLISH]. That's only about 18 kilometers from Malinsk. And this is my grandparents from my mother's parents, Peakman. And the picture was-- it must've been taken in 1926, or maybe around that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Berezne. OK, sitting holding the baby is my father, Gold Perlmutter. Next to him sitting, my mother, Sarah Perlmutter. Next to her standing is my half-brother, Enoch Serebrenick, my mother's son. Behind her is my brother, Avemu Perlmutter. The woman, the girl is my sister, Panina Perlmutter. Next to her is standing my brother, Surlick Serebrenick, which is my half-brother from my mother's side. Now the one who is standing beside is me, Moishe, and then Gershen is sitting on my father. So those are those. Now the two brothers, which I mentioned Serebrenick from my mother's, one never got out from Russia because of a Russian Jew stole his Polish papers. He left and they wouldn't let him out. So he remained, and he died in Russia. My other brother, Henry died in Miami. He came out of Russia and was in Germany. And from Germany came to New York. Then he lived in Buffalo, and he lived in Miami. He died a few years back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did your other brother die?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: The one who died in Russia, when did he die?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they died six months apart one another. The one in Miami died before, and six months later my brother in Russia died. Yeah. OK, this is a picture, in front of the building is [NON-ENGLISH], that's Negrud. In the middle, which is holding there a little boy, or she's holding somebody, is Eleanor Kichler, which she ran the hall. There's the [NON-ENGLISH] place, the orphanage. And next to it is a woman there who is a teacher. And next to her on the right of her-- on the left of her-- is me. On the other side-- on the end of the picture, there's a guy there which without an eye. He had 21 pieces in his body. Nathan-- there was a bomb exploded and he had-- he survived and he lived. And the others, there's lots of-- there's [NON-ENGLISH] Neuman, which he died in 1948 in Israel. The woman that I said, Edith. Edith is right next to Kichler. And all the others-- a lot of names that I don't consider. There's Leanne and there's all kinds. Yeah, that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, in the picture-- oh, yeah. That's Izzy and I. It was [NON-ENGLISH] in 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: Left to right, which ones is Izzy, which is you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you see, right was Izzy and left is me. Izzy with the hat on, I without the hat. If you wouldn't recognize. Izzy is the one who died in 1948 in Israel. OK, this is a picture of 1969, 21st of October. Like the man says, I should never forget it. So I remember. That's my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Marcka Perlmutter, now. [NON-ENGLISH] from before. She was Marcka Krumush. Now she's Marcka Perlmutter. There. That's it. OK, this is my whole family. My wife, my lovely wife. And to my left behind me is my oldest son, Shai. And Gadi, the middle one. And the last one, which is Roz. And they're all doing pretty well. I'm very proud of them. And let Shy take over from then to tell whatever he wants to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife's name is Marcka. I mentioned that before. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi, my name's Shai. My two brothers, Gad and Roz. Gad and I, we work for ourselves. We have our own company training animals. And Roz works for himself. He works for a company, he's on a computer, and does very well for himself. The only things I really have to say is I'm really proud of my parents. I'm glad they raised us well. I'm really glad my father had a chance to tell his stories. We've heard them over and over again. And he wanted an opportunity to tell everybody also his stories. Thank you.
